1: Timnah (modern Khirbet Tibnah) lay in the Sorek Valley about 8 km northwest of Samson's hometown of Zorah, on the boundary between Israelite-held hills and Philistine-controlled lowlands. The Sorek Valley was a natural corridor from the coastal plain into the Shephelah — geographically, economically, and ethnically a contested frontier zone. Samson's downward movement (wayyēred, he went down) is both geographical (from Zorah's hills to the valley) and narratively ominous: every Samson episode involving a woman involves descent.
2: Ancient Israelite marriage was arranged through the father, who negotiated the môhar (bride-price) and entered a binding covenant with the bride's family. Requesting a foreign wife violated both the cultural expectation of endogamy (marriage within the clan or tribe) and the specific Deuteronomic prohibition against covenanting with the Canaanite peoples (Deut 7:1-4). While Philistines were not technically Canaanites, the principle of separation applied. Samson bypasses normal protocol by reporting the request himself rather than sending his father to negotiate — itself a social irregularity.
3: 'Uncircumcised Philistines' (ʾarlê pĕlištîm) — circumcision was the physical covenant-marker distinguishing Israel from surrounding nations. The Philistines were the paradigmatic uncircumcised people in the Hebrew Bible (1 Sam 17:26, 36; 2 Sam 1:20), and the phrase became a term of contempt. The Philistines arrived as Sea Peoples from the Aegean around 1200 BCE and did not practice circumcision, unlike most Semitic neighbors. His father and mother's question appeals to both covenant logic (don't mix with those who bear no covenant sign) and practical wisdom (available women within the extended family).
4: The narrator's explanatory comment — 'this was from the LORD, who was seeking an occasion against the Philistines' — addresses a theological problem that ancient readers would have noticed: how can YHWH use Samson's sinful desire for a foreign woman? The ANE background is the concept of divine hardening used throughout the Hebrew Bible (Exod 4:21; 1 Sam 2:25; 1 Kgs 22:22) — YHWH works within and through human choices, even sinful ones, to accomplish sovereign purposes. This does not exculpate Samson; it places his narrative within the larger framework of YHWH's war against Philistine oppression.
5: The route to Timnah passed through the vineyards of Timnah — the Sorek Valley was wine-producing territory (the name Sorek means 'choice grapevine'). A vineyard attack by a young lion (kĕpîr ʾărāyôt, a mature young lion capable of independent hunting) was a real danger in the ancient Levant; lions inhabited the Jordan River basin and surrounding terrain well into the monarchic period and later. Samson's separation from his parents for the encounter preserves the secret: he tells neither parent what he has done (v.6,9).
6: Killing a lion barehanded was the paradigmatic act of heroic valor in ANE warrior culture. Heracles was depicted killing the Nemean lion; David claims lion-killing as his qualification for facing Goliath (1 Sam 17:34-36); Benaiah son of Jehoiada is celebrated for killing a lion in a pit on a snowy day (2 Sam 23:20). The Spirit of YHWH rushing upon Samson (wattillaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH) is the same empowerment formula used at Othniel (3:10), Gideon (6:34), and Jephthah (11:29) — but here it enables a feat of raw physical strength rather than military leadership. Samson's silence about it shows he is not performing for honor, at least in this instance.
7: Samson's conversation with the woman (wayyiḏabber ʿim-hāʾiššāh) follows the betrothal negotiation. The phrase wayyîšar Šimšôn bĕʿênāyw (she pleased Samson, literally 'she was right/straight in his eyes') uses the same root as the Judges refrain 'everyone did what was right in his own eyes' (hayyāšār bĕʿênāyw): what Samson finds straight and right is determined by personal desire, the book's definition of the problem.
8: The return to Timnah to complete the marriage is accompanied by the curiosity of checking the dead lion's carcass. The time gap ('after some time') suggests weeks or months. Contact with a dead body (nepeš mēt) was ritually polluting for all Israelites (Num 19:11-16) and doubly prohibited for Nazirites (Num 6:6-7: 'he shall not approach a dead body'). The honeybees settling in a lion's carcass is an unusual but not impossible event — bees occasionally colonize protected hollow spaces including dry cavities. Samson scoops the honey with his hands, both touching the carcass and potentially consuming something from an unclean source.
9: Samson brings the honey to his parents without disclosing its source — the narrator's emphasis on their eating it without knowing (wĕhēmmāh lōʾ yādĕʿû) implicates them in the defilement. The honey episode sets up the riddle: only someone who knew the specific source could solve it. In ANE wisdom tradition, an impossible riddle — one requiring inside knowledge — was a legitimate intellectual challenge but also a form of deception when used in a wager. Samson's riddle is designed to be unsolvable from the outside.
10: The wedding feast (mišteh, H4960 — a drinking-feast, from šātāh, to drink) was a standard ANE institution lasting seven days for a principal wedding. Jacob served a week-long feast at his marriage to Leah (Gen 29:27-28). The feast was hosted at the bride's location (the bride's family's responsibility in Philistine custom here), with the bridegroom and his party as guests. The logistics involved significant hospitality expense — feeding and entertaining a large party for a week was a matter of community honor.
11: The Philistines provide thirty companions (šĕlōšîm mērēʿîm) for Samson. In some ANE wedding contexts, groomsmen were provided by the host community to honor the guest and also to keep watch over a potentially dangerous visitor. Samson's superhuman reputation may have been known — the 30 men serve as both honor-guard and surveillance. This number will become the stake in the riddle wager and then the source of Samson's murderous retaliation in v.19.
12: Riddle contests at feasts were a recognized feature of ANE symposium culture, attested in Egyptian and Ugaritic texts. A successful riddler demonstrated superior intelligence and access to hidden knowledge. The wager — 30 linen garments and 30 changes of clothing — represented significant wealth: fine linen was an expensive import, and 60 items of clothing was a substantial wardrobe by ancient standards. The seven-day time limit matches the feast's duration, making the contest a formal feature of the wedding entertainment.
13: The counter-wager is exactly symmetrical — 30 garments each way. The balance of the wager is a matter of honor: neither side can appear to be gambling unfairly. The public nature of the challenge, made in front of all thirty companions, means losing involves communal shame. The linen garments (sĕdînîm, H5466) were a luxury item imported from Egypt or Phoenicia; 'changes of clothing' (ḥălîpôt bĕgādîm) were formal dress sets for different occasions, not everyday work clothing.
14: The riddle — 'Out of the eater came food; out of the strong came sweetness' — is structurally insoluble without knowing the lion-honey incident. ANE riddle tradition distinguished between fair riddles (solvable by logic or knowledge the audience possesses) and impossible riddles (requiring inside information). Using an impossible riddle as a wager was considered by some as a form of sharp dealing. The companions' three-day failure (v.14b) establishes that no amount of conventional reasoning can crack it.
15: The escalation from intellectual pressure to death threat — 'coax your husband or we will burn you and your father's house with fire' — reflects the honor-violence logic of the ancient world: the 30 men have staked public honor on solving the riddle, and failure means communal shame. Burning someone's house was a recognized instrument of tribal punishment (Judg 12:1; 15:6); the threat is credible. The seven-day duration also puts the bride's family in the impossible position: hospitality requires protecting guests, but the threat endangers their own household.
16: Samson's wife's accusation — 'You only hate me, you don't love me' — employs the emotional rhetoric of the ANE domestic sphere. The claim reverses the expected covenant loyalty: she inverts the love-proof demand (if you loved me, you would tell me). The same emotional logic appears in Delilah's successful appeal (16:15). This is the first demonstration of a pattern in the Samson narrative: women use intimate relationship as leverage to extract covenant secrets from him.
17: The seven days of weeping correspond exactly to the seven-day feast — the pressure is maintained for the entire duration. 'Because she pressed him hard' (kî ṣārĕrāh-lô, she was a narrow/pressing-place to him) — the verb ṣārar means to be in straits, to be hemmed in; she made his situation feel desperate. The social and emotional pressure of the feast context — surrounded by Philistine companions, unable to avoid his wife's weeping, in enemy territory — explains why Samson breaks where he does.
18: The companions' answer — 'What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' — technically answers the riddle by giving natural referents, but Samson recognizes it as derived knowledge. His response ('if you had not plowed with my heifer') uses agricultural metaphor: ʿeglāh (H5697, heifer) refers to his wife, and ḥaraštEM (plowed) refers to sexual/intimate access. The agricultural idiom for sexual knowledge is common in the ANE (Song 4:12-16 uses garden imagery similarly). The deadline 'before the sun set' confirms the legal precision of the wager: sunset ended the seventh day.
19: Ashkelon was one of the five Philistine city-states (the Pentapolis: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath), located on the Mediterranean coast about 38 km southwest of Timnah. It was a major port and commercial center, with a dense population. The Spirit of YHWH empowers Samson to kill thirty Ashkelonites — strangers entirely unconnected to the riddle dispute — to pay the wager. The moral problem this raises is left unaddressed by the narrator: the Spirit-empowerment is noted without editorial comment. Samson takes the garments and pays the debt, then departs 'in anger' (ḥărî ʾappô, burning of his nostrils).
20: The bride given to the companion (mērēaʿ, best man or closest companion) was legally possible in the ANE when a husband abandoned his wife without formal divorce. The Hammurabi Code (§137-138) and other ANE legal texts address the question of abandoned wives; remarriage to another man was recognized after a period of abandonment. From the Philistine father's perspective, Samson left without consummating the marriage publicly and without formal divorce, making the transfer legally reasonable. For Samson, this represents a double betrayal: his wife betrayed his secret, and the Philistines gave her away.
